title: Frank B. Hu | Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health
excerpts:
Section Title: Frank B. Hu > Biography
Content:
Dr. Frank Hu is Chair of Department of Nutrition, Fredrick J. Stare Professor of Nutrition and Epidemiology at Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health and Professor of Medicine, Harvard Medical School and Brigham and Women’s Hospital. Dr. Hu received his MD from Tongji Medical College in China and MPH and PhD in Epidemiology from University of Illinois at Chicago. He completed a postdoctoral fellowship in Nutritional Epidemiology at Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health. Dr. Hu’s major research interests include epidemiology and prevention of cardiometabolic diseases through diet and lifestyle; gene-environment interactions; nutritional omics and precision nutrition; and nutrition transitions in low- and middle-income countries. Currently, he serves as Director of Dietary Biomarker Development Center at Harvard University. He has published a textbook on Obesity Epidemiology (Oxford University Press) and >1500 peer-reviewed papers wi

Oct 20, 2025 — ... Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health, where he currently serves as chair of the Department of Nutrition. Frank B. Hu with shoulder

Section Title: About the Editor: Frank B. Hu, MD, MPH, PhD—Understanding the Effects of Diet and Lifestyle on Ca...
Content:
He received his medical degree from Tongji Medical College, in his native Wuhan, China, then landed a fellowship at The University of Hong Kong Li Ka Shing Faculty of Medicine. There, budding health trends sparked his interest in epidemiology and cardiometabolic diseases.
“In the early ‘90s, as China experienced rapid economic development, it also saw a huge increase in the prevalence of chronic diseases, including obesity, diabetes, and cardiovascular disease,” Hu says. “I was fascinated by those changes and felt it was important to understand the root causes.”
He earned his MPH and PhD in epidemiology from the University of Illinois at Chicago and then completed a postdoctoral fellowship in nutritional epidemiology at the Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health, where he currently serves as chair of the Department of Nutrition.

Section Title: Harvard Catalyst Profiles > Frank B. Hu, M.D., Ph.D.
Content:
He has published a textbook on Obesity Epidemiology (Oxford University Press) and >1500 peer-reviewed papers with an H-index of 320. He served on the Institute of Medicine (IOM) Committee on Preventing the Global Epidemic of Cardiovascular Disease, the Obesity Guideline Expert Panel, American Heart Association Nutrition Committee, and the 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee, USDA/HHS. He has served on the editorial boards of Lancet Diabetes & Endocrinology, Diabetes Care, and Clinical Chemistry. Dr. Hu was elected to the National Academy of Medicine in 2015.

Section Title: Harvard Catalyst Profiles > Frank B. Hu, M.D., Ph.D.
Content:
Dr. Frank Hu is Chair of Department of Nutrition, Fredrick J. Stare Professor of Nutrition and Epidemiology at Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health and Professor of Medicine, Harvard Medical School and Brigham and Women’s Hospital. Dr. Hu received his MD from Tongji Medical College in China and MPH and PhD in Epidemiology from University of Illinois at Chicago. He completed a postdoctoral fellowship in Nutritional Epidemiology at Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health. Dr. Hu’s major research interests include epidemiology and prevention of cardiometabolic diseases through diet and lifestyle; gene-environment interactions; nutritional omics and precision nutrition; and nutrition transitions in low- and middle-income countries. Currently, he serves as Director of Dietary Biomarker Development Center at Harvard University.

title: Is Carb-Cutting Healthy? - The New York Times
excerpts:
Section Title: Why Many Doctors Don’t Like Low-Carb Diets > What Low-Carb Eating Can and Can’t Do
Content:
“Traditional low-carb diets are loaded with red meat,” said Dr. Frank B. Hu, a professor of nutrition and epidemiology at the Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health. And [too much saturated fat](https://www.nytimes.com/2025/12/09/well/eat/saturated-fat-health-risks-dietary-guidelines.html) can increase the risk of developing Type 2 diabetes and cardiovascular disease, he added.
If you cut out starchy vegetables and most fruits, legumes and whole grains while on a low-carbohydrate diet, that could also mean missing out on beneficial fiber, vitamins and other nutrients that are important for cardiovascular health.
Dr. Hu’s research has found that a diet low in total carbohydrates but relatively high in whole grains, healthy sources of fat and plant-based proteins can help you lose weight and [slow weight gain in the long run](https://jamanetwork.com/journals/jamanetworkopen/fulla

title: Frank B. Hu, M.D., Ph.D. | HuffPost
excerpts:
Section Title: Frank B. Hu, M.D., Ph.D.
Content:
Professor of Nutrition and Epidemiology at Harvard School of Public Health; Professor of Medicine at Harvard Medical School
Dr. Frank B. Hu, M.D., Ph.D., is Professor of Nutrition and Epidemiology at Harvard School of Public Health (HSPH) and Professor of Medicine at Harvard Medical School. Dr. Hu is Co-director of the Program in Obesity Epidemiology and Prevention at HSPH. He is also the Director of Harvard Transdisciplinary Research on Energetics and Cancer (TREC) center. His research is mainly focused on dietary and lifestyle determinants of obesity and Type 2 diabetes. His group has conducted extensive analyses on sleep deprivation and shift work and risk of obesity and diabetes in the Nurses’ Health Study. Dr. Hu has published more than 400 original papers and reviews and a textbook on Obesity Epidemiology (Oxford University Press 2008).
